affected_groups: Disadvantaged pupils in the general-school (comprehensive) sector excluded from selective tracks
General-school teachers and pupils benefiting from workload-reducing and autonomy-enhancing elements of the scenario
Policymakers and finance ministry balancing fiscal consolidation against targeted equity funding commitments
content.decision_question: Equity vs. incrementalism: structural fairness in access to selective tracks versus the political feasibility of compensating within an unchanged selection system
content.evidence_boundary: Partially settled, partially contested: the scenario's own equity_impact field acknowledges bounded effectiveness due to unaddressed selection age (a structural fact), and fiscal-consolidation risk to targeted funding is documented (6.2% deficit 2026); however, whether compensatory investment can close (not merely reduce) achievement gaps relative to non-TEIP general-school cohorts is empirically unknown due to absent longitudinal evidence, and Portugal's 0.3 SD gain, while real, is not directly transferable evidence about interaction with early selection since Portugal's system does not track this early
content.tension: Non-structural compensation leaves the early-selection mechanism that produces near-zero disadvantaged-pupil admission to selective tracks untouched, structurally bounding its equity effect and risking legitimizing segregation while claiming to address it.
content.title: Decision tension A12: S4
value_poles: incrementalism: structural fairness in access to selective tracks versus the political feasibility of compensating within an unchanged selection system
